title: New Zealand Winegrowers welcomes support for the India Free Trade Agreement
summary:
New Zealand Winegrowers has welcomed the announcement of Labour’s support for progressing a Free Trade Agreement between New Zealand and India, “reinforcing trade as a shared priority”. Philip Gregan, CEO of New Zealand Winegrowers, said maintenance of bipartisan alignment on trade is very positive as it gives exporters certainty and reinforces New Zealand’s reputation as a long-term trading partner. “India represents a significant long-term opportunity for New Zealand wine, and sustained cross-party commitment will be key to delivering a high-quality agreement with real commercial outcomes,” he said.

The post New Zealand Winegrowers welcomes support for the India Free Trade Agreement appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
New Zealand Winegrowers has shown support for progressing a Free Trade Agreement with India. Image courtesy New Zealand Winegrowers

New Zealand Winegrowers has welcomed the announcement of Labour’s support for progressing a Free Trade Agreement between New Zealand and India, “reinforcing trade as a shared priority”.

Philip Gregan, CEO of New Zealand Winegrowers, said maintenance of bipartisan alignment on trade is very positive as it gives exporters certainty and reinforces New Zealand’s reputation as a long-term trading partner.

“India represents a significant long-term opportunity for New Zealand wine, and sustained cross-party commitment will be key to delivering a high-quality agreement with real commercial outcomes,” he said.

New Zealand Winegrowers views the FTA as positive news for the industry as wineries will be able to plan with confidence around a clear, decade-long tariff reduction pathway.

While it noted that current exports to India are very modest, sitting at just under NZ $300,000 (FOB) for 2025 due to the very high tariffs, “improved market access under the FTA is expected to make India increasingly attractive to New Zealand wine exporters over the coming years”.

“We value the constructive approach taken across Parliament and the shared recognition of trade as a driver of growth, jobs, and regional prosperity,” Gregan said.

The post New Zealand Winegrowers welcomes support for the India Free Trade Agreement appeared first on Winetitles.

title: Riverland Wine appoints new executive officer
summary:
Riverland Wine has appointed Andrew Weeks as its new executive officer, effective Monday 27 April 2026. Riverland Wine said that Weeks brings a “deep understanding of the winegrape sector shaped by a career spanning viticulture, business management, national industry advocacy, and with a personal connection to the Riverland that goes back to his childhood in Renmark”. “The Riverland has always been central to Australia’s wine industry, and I’m proud to be returning to the region in this role. Growers and winemakers here are doing it tough, and they deserve strong, practical representation. I’m looking forward to working alongside the board, members and industry partners to push for the settings that will keep this region viable and thriving for the long term,” Weeks said.

The post Riverland Wine appoints new executive officer appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Andrew Weeks will assume the position from next Monday. Image supplied

Riverland Wine has appointed Andrew Weeks as its new executive officer, effective Monday 27 April 2026.

Riverland Wine said that Weeks brings a “deep understanding of the winegrape sector shaped by a career spanning viticulture, business management, national industry advocacy, and with a personal connection to the Riverland that goes back to his childhood in Renmark”.

“The Riverland has always been central to Australia’s wine industry, and I’m proud to be returning to the region in this role. Growers and winemakers here are doing it tough, and they deserve strong, practical representation.

“I’m looking forward to working alongside the board, members and industry partners to push for the settings that will keep this region viable and thriving for the long term,” Weeks said.

As CEO of Wine Grape Growers Australia, the national advocacy body, Week’s led the organisation’s transition to become Australian Vignerons which bestowed him with experience in policy, biosecurity, and research and extension at a national level.

As senior viticulture officer at CCW Co-operative, he provided technical advice to a group that accounted for around 10% of the nations annual grape crush. He also holds a Bachelor of Agricultural Science from the University of Adelaide and has lectured in wine business at Flinders University.

Brigid Nolan, chair of Riverland Wine, said that Andrew is exactly the right person for the role right now.

“His grounding in viticulture, his experience in national advocacy, and his genuine connection to the Riverland mean he understands both the technical and commercial realities our growers and winemakers face every day.

“We look forward to having him lead our advocacy with the energy and focus this region deserves,” she said.

Riverland Wine said that Week’s will work directly with government, regulators and industry partners to ensure the Riverland’s challenges, and its essential contribution to the national wine sector are properly understood and addressed.

His appointment follows the departure of outgoing executive officer Alex Cannon-Leyson, who finished her tenure on 20 March 2026.

Riverland Wine extended thanks to Cannon-Leyson for her dedication and significant contribution to the organisation during a period of extraordinary challenge and change.

The post Riverland Wine appoints new executive officer appeared first on Winetitles.

title: WISA farewells committee members and confirms 2026 leadership
summary:
The Wine Industry Suppliers Association (WISA) has acknowledged the contribution of its departing committee members while confirming its leadership team. “We formally acknowledge the individuals contributing to our management committee and the critical role volunteer leadership plays in representing and advancing the wine value chain,” WISA said. The organisation also confirmed that Amy Bishop (DW Fox Tucker) and Anna Lindsay (Rural Freight Connections) are stepping down from the committee, with Steven Scott (Vinarchy) having resigned at the end of 2025.

The post WISA farewells committee members and confirms 2026 leadership appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The 2026 WISA committee. Image supplied

The Wine Industry Suppliers Association (WISA) has acknowledged the contribution of its departing committee members while confirming its leadership team.

“We formally acknowledge the individuals contributing to our management committee and the critical role volunteer leadership plays in representing and advancing the wine value chain,” WISA said.

The organisation also confirmed that Amy Bishop (DW Fox Tucker) and Anna Lindsay (Rural Freight Connections) are stepping down from the committee, with Steven Scott (Vinarchy) having resigned at the end of 2025.

Bishop served for over six years, including as secretary and public officer, while Scott was with WISA for four years and Lindsay for one.

“We thank all for their contribution to WISA and look forward to staying connected following the strong relationships built during their time with the association. All remain WISA members and continue to support the value chain through their respective businesses,” it clarified.

WISA said it is proud to welcome Athena Stamoulos, associate from Finlaysons, who joined the committee in April following Bishop’s departure.

“Stamoulos adds critical legal and commercial capability to WISA’s committee, supporting strong governance and future-focused decision-making.”

Executive chair Liz Schoen acknowledged that the contribution of volunteer committee members remains fundamental to WISA’s ability to deliver value to members and the broader industry.

“WISA exists to champion the wine value chain, and that only happens because of the individuals who step forward to contribute their time and expertise.

“Our committee members are not removed from the challenges of the industry, they are living them in their own businesses. That makes their contribution both practical and highly relevant.

“Following the transition of WineTech and the evolution of WISA’s operating model, the role of the committee has become even more important in shaping a sustainable and commercially relevant future for supplier businesses,” she said.

WISA affirmed that it continues to focus on strengthening its role as the voice of suppliers, with initiatives spanning advocacy, professional development, industry events and collaboration across the value chain.

“We remain committed to building stronger connections between suppliers and producers, while ensuring supplier businesses maintain influence, visibility and commercial relevance within the Australian and New Zealand wine sector,” WISA explained.

The 2026 committee is as follows:

  • Liz Schoen (WISA executive chair & Liz Schoen Consulting)
  • Eric Wilkes (Australian Wine Research Institute) – deputy chair
  • Ron van Buuren (Hydra Consulting) – treasurer
  • Romina Meloni (Lallemand)
  • Tim Stead (Amorim)
  • Melissa Raymond (Garden & Field)
  • Aaron Haw (Cork Supply)
  • Athena Stamoulos (Finlaysons)
  • Adrian Fiocchini (Seguin Moreau)

title: GIN Virus detected in Australia for the first time
summary:
Vinehealth Australia has today issued a biosecurity alert to advise the grapegrowing industry that Grapevine Berry Inner Necrosis Virus (GIN Virus/GINV/ Trichovirus necroacini) has been detected in vineyards across Victoria, Queensland and South Australia. These detections are the first time GIN Virus has been recorded and reported in Australia, however, Vinehealth said that the virus is likely to have been present in Australia for some years. It is likely that the virus propagated and distributed in plant material across eastern Australia, therefore, Vinehealth concluded that eradication is not possible. According to Vinehealth, during late 2025 and early 2026 the virus was detected in table grapevines and rootstocks that were diseased yet asymptomatic in grapegrowing regions within Victoria, Queensland and South Australia.

The post GIN Virus detected in Australia for the first time appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Vinehealth Australia has today issued a biosecurity alert to advise the grapegrowing industry that Grapevine Berry Inner Necrosis Virus (GIN Virus/GINV/ Trichovirus necroacini) has been detected in vineyards across Victoria, Queensland and South Australia.

These detections are the first time GIN Virus has been recorded and reported in Australia, however, Vinehealth said that the virus is likely to have been present in Australia for some years. It is likely that the virus propagated and distributed in plant material across eastern Australia, therefore, Vinehealth concluded that eradication is not possible.

According to Vinehealth, during late 2025 and early 2026 the virus was detected in table grapevines and rootstocks that were diseased yet asymptomatic in grapegrowing regions within Victoria, Queensland and South Australia.

Asymptomatic mature grapevines samples from South Australian table grapes returned positive results to GIN Virus in 2026. The extent of the virus spread is currently unknown.

Vinehealth Australia explained that some infected vines in Australia displayed restricted growth in Spring, however, it is not possible to confirm if this was due to the virus or other contributing factors causing the reduced growth. As the season progresses, vines may grow out and appear normal. Asymptomatic infections also occur, meaning an infected vine may not show any visual symptoms of the virus.

“The disease is established in China and Japan where it is known to impact the health and productivity of sensitive varieties. Overseas experience has shown that symptoms vary across different varieties.

“Leaf symptoms have been recorded in some GIN Virus infected wine and table grapes and rootstocks, but asymptomatic infections also occur. Impacts on fruit production are only recorded in table grapes. Although some winegrape varieties may show leaf symptoms, it is unknown if GIN Virus will significantly impact fruit quality and yield or sensory differences on resulting wines.

“Visual symptoms can include delayed budburst, stunted growth, zig zag shoots and affected shoots may have internal necrosis. Leaves may be smaller and can show yellow blotches, mosaic-like patterns or small rings. Bunches may be more compact and unusually tight, berries may be smaller, ripen slowly, and have reduced grape quality including reduced sugar and acid balance. Berries can have inner necrosis, which gives the virus its name, but can also exhibit necrosis on the outside,” Vinehealth Australia detailed in the alert.

Currently, laboratory analysis is the only method known to identify GIN Virus.

“The virus can also be transmitted via grafting of infected planting material. The grape erineum mite (blister and bud mite) Colomerus vitis transmits the GIN Virus between grapevines.”

There is no known cure for infected vines and no published information on the economic impact of GIN Virus at this stage. The speed and risk of spread is also currently unknown.

“Site quarantine action plans for the infected properties in Australia are being refined. It is expected the infected properties will be required to undertake strict farmgate hygiene to prevent the spread to other adjoining and contact vineyards.

“Industry is conducting further investigations and surveillance to better understand this virus and how it can be managed to minimise the impact,” Vinehealth Australia explained.

To prevent GIN Virus entering the vineyard, Vinehealth encourages the prioritisation of farmgate hygiene by practicing ‘clean in and clean out’. Controlling vectors/mites can help slow the spread to adjoining vines.

Grapegrowers should purchase known GIN Virus-free planting material and ask for evidence of testing.

“Various chemical, biological and cultural control methods in line with integrated pest management can be adopted to encourage predatory insects into your vineyard. Check with the AWRI ‘Dog Book’ Agrochemicals Booklet for current chemical strategies for mite control. Some fungicides will reduce predator populations and allow mites to flourish, increasing the risk of GIN Virus spread.  Use insecticides in line with good Integrated Pest Management practices (IPM).

“It is unclear if other insect vectors, such as other mites, scale and mealybugs, may spread the virus. Additional vectors may include people, plant, soil, machinery and equipment where insect vectors may hitchhike on these items.”

If a vineyard has GIN Virus, the workers should prioritise farmgate hygiene, use clean grapevine material, work in uninfected vineyards before moving to infected vineyards, and clean machinery between vineyards to prevent the spread.

Overseas, GIN Virus has been known to infect winegrape varieties, although there is no evidence in Australia that winegrapes have been infected thus far.

Virus testing can be undertaken at:

Agriculture Victoria: (03) 9032 7515 or chs.reception@agriculture.vic.gov.au

New South Wales Department of Primary Industries and Regional Development: 1800 675 623 or laboratory.services@dpird.nsw.gov.au

Vinehealth Australia encourages anyone with questions to contact them on (08) 8273 0550 or email admin@vinehealth.com.au, or call the Vinehealth Australia CEO, Dr Samantha Scarratt, on 0436 415 611.

Vinehealth Australia said it will update South Australian vineyard owners once further information is known.

The post GIN Virus detected in Australia for the first time appeared first on Winetitles.

title: Calabria unifies domestic distribution under House of Fine Wine
summary:
Calabria Family Wine Group has confirmed House of Fine Wine as its unified domestic trading and distribution platform. The group said that this creates a simpler, more streamlined way for the market to engage with the group’s portfolio. The move follows the group’s acquisition of House of Fine Wine and The Wine Company back in February, “marking the next phase of integration, bringing its domestic distribution businesses together under a single, premium, market-facing identity”.

The post Calabria unifies domestic distribution under House of Fine Wine appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
House of Fine Wine will be Calabria’s unified domestic trading and distribution platform. Image supplied

Calabria Family Wine Group has confirmed House of Fine Wine as its unified domestic trading and distribution platform.

The group said that this creates a simpler, more streamlined way for the market to engage with the group’s portfolio.

The move follows the group’s acquisition of House of Fine Wine and The Wine Company back in February, “marking the next phase of integration, bringing its domestic distribution businesses together under a single, premium, market-facing identity”.

The winery said that by operating under one banner, it will strengthen its national reach and capability across on-premise and independent retail channels.

Third-generation family member Andrew Calabria, sales and marketing director, said the move reflects the group’s long-term vision for a more cohesive and scalable distribution model.

“This is a natural and exciting next step following our recent acquisitions. By bringing our distribution businesses together under the House of Fine Wine name, we’re creating greater clarity for the market while building a platform that supports growth, investment and long-term partnerships, all while remaining proudly family owned,” he said.

Simon Limmer, CEO of Indevin Group, owner of the Villa Maria brand, said the unified platform will support the brands continued growth in Australia.

“Calabria Family Wine Group brings deep market expertise, with a strong distribution platform that ensures greater consistency in how brands are represented.

“We’re confident this next phase will strengthen execution for Villa Maria in Australia and support continued investment in long-term brand building with the trade,” he said.

The post Calabria unifies domestic distribution under House of Fine Wine appeared first on Winetitles.

title: TWE flags operational changes amidst company restructure
summary:
In an ASX announcement, Treasury Wine Estates (TWE) has today announced the transition to a new “regional operating model” amidst the progression of its global transformation program, TWE Ascent, aiming to “position the business for long-term sustainable growth”. The regional operating model will be structured across the Americas, Australia and New Zealand (ANZ), Europe, Greater China, and emerging markets such as the rest of Asia, Middle East and Africa. It will come into effect 1 October 2026. TWE said it is focusing on creating a unified, simplified portfolio for “increased focus and execution at a market level”. Further details will be provided at TWE’s Investors Day on 4 June 2026.

The post TWE flags operational changes amidst company restructure appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The winery has announced its new regional operating model. Image courtesy Penfolds

In an ASX announcement, Treasury Wine Estates (TWE) has today announced the transition to a new “regional operating model” amidst the progression of its global transformation program, TWE Ascent, aiming to “position the business for long-term sustainable growth”.

The regional operating model will be structured across the Americas, Australia and New Zealand (ANZ), Europe, Greater China, and emerging markets such as the rest of Asia, Middle East and Africa. It will come into effect 1 October 2026.

TWE said it is focusing on creating a unified, simplified portfolio for “increased focus and execution at a market level”. Further details will be provided at TWE’s Investors Day on 4 June 2026.

 

Market depletions

The company has seen “improved depletions performance in 3Q26”, noting “strong depletions growth” in China “driven by the ongoing demand for Bin 389 and Bin 407”. TWE said that performance through the Chinese New Year period was a highlight, with “depletions up 40% in China on a seasonally adjusted basis and momentum continuing to the end of 3Q26”.

In ANZ, 3Q26 depletions grew 11% and in Asia (excluding China), depletions grew 14% on a seasonally adjusted basis according to TWE.

Treasury Americas overall US depletions grew 9.1% in 3Q26, “reflecting improved momentum”.

The winery said that the positive depletions momentum is expected to support its focus on reducing customer inventory levels in China and the US.

 

Funding update

TWE said that as a part of its ongoing funding program, it has “established new debt commitments” totalling $300 million “from a number of lenders within its global banking group”. The company said these commitments will be used to refinance F27 debt maturities and further increase TWE’s liquidity position, which it claims is expected to exceed $1 billion by the end of F26. The winery said it retains “significant headroom to the financial covenants under its borrowing arrangements and remains confident in its ability to return leverage to the target 1.5-2.0x range”.

TWE CEO Sam Fischer said that the company is reshaping to drive clearer accountability for performance and to enable faster, more market-connected decision-making.

“Combining the deep local insight of our in-market teams with the scale and expertise of our global functions will step change in-market execution, whilst retaining our enhanced focus on Penfolds and other priority luxury brands.

“I am pleased with the progress we are making on elevating our focus on depletions performance across our key markets, and we remain focused on continuing the improved momentum,” he said.

 

‘Executive Leadership Team’ changes

TWE will realign its ‘Executive Leadership Team’ (ELT), driving the organisation transition to the new operating model, effective 1 October 2026.

Tom King, who is currently managing director for Penfolds, will assume a new position as chief commercial officer. He will report to CEO Sam Fischer and lead the regional sales, marketing, direct to consumer and commercial strategy activities for all regions except the Americas. He will be based in Melbourne.

Reporting to Tom King, Angus Lilley, who is currently managing director for Treasury Collective, will lead the ANZ and Europe regions “with accountability for regional sales and marketing activities” for TWE’s focused brand portfolio from Melbourne.

Jack Wu, currently general manager Mainland China for Penfolds, will lead the Greater China region, based in Shanghai.

Kirsty Keyte, current chief marketing officer for Penfolds, will become the new position of chief marketing and innovation officer. She will be based in Melbourne and report to Tom King, being responsible for global marketing and the delivery of global brand strategy.

Also reporting to Fischer will be Ben Dollard, current president of Treasury Americas. He will lead TWE’s Americas region with responsibility for sales, marketing, direct to consumer and commercial strategy activities.

“The direct CEO reporting line reflects heightened focus on TWE’s Americas business, providing direct line of sight into in-market execution in the Americas. A dotted reporting line into the CCO ensures a consistent and disciplined execution framework across all regions including the Americas,” said TWE.

Kerrin Petty will remain chief supply and sustainability officer, with an expanded global supply remit, including Americas supply. Petty will report to Fischer and be based in Adelaide.

Group functions reporting to CEO Fischer include the chief financial and strategy officer, chief legal and corporate relations officer (incorporating the legal, corporate affairs and company secretariat functions), chief people officer and chief digital and technology officer.

The post TWE flags operational changes amidst company restructure appeared first on Winetitles.

title: ANU PhD student awarded for vineyard pest research
summary:
A new project led by Australian National University (ANU) PhD candidate Yilin Bai is targeting two significant pests to Australian vineyards. Bai has subsequently been awarded Wine Australia’s Dr Tony Jordan OAM Award for 2026. Named in honour of the late Dr Tony Jordan OAM, the award recognises “the most outstanding applicant of Wine Australia’s annual PhD and Master by Research top-up scholarship program”. A key part of Bai’s research is developing an easy and reliable way to accurately identify scale insect and mealybug species using machine learning and genetic tools. The project will also use genomic methods to track how the pests spread across vineyards.

The post ANU PhD student awarded for vineyard pest research appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Yinlin Bai, the PhD candidate leading the pest research. Image supplied

A new project led by Australian National University (ANU) PhD candidate Yilin Bai is targeting two significant pests to Australian vineyards.

Bai has subsequently been awarded Wine Australia’s Dr Tony Jordan OAM Award for 2026. Named in honour of the late Dr Tony Jordan OAM, the award recognises “the most outstanding applicant of Wine Australia’s annual PhD and Master by Research top-up scholarship program”.

A key part of Bai’s research is developing an easy and reliable way to accurately identify scale insect and mealybug species using machine learning and genetic tools. The project will also use genomic methods to track how the pests spread across vineyards.

Wine Australia described how scale insects and mealybugs can damage grapevines by feeding on plant sap, which can reduce vine vigour and slow growth. As the pests feed, they excrete honeydew that coats leaves and grape bunches, encouraging sooty mould which reduces photosynthesis and contaminates fruit.

This contamination can be devastating, leading to downgraded or rejected grapes at harvest. Scale insects and mealybugs are also known vectors of grapevine viruses, further compounding their potential impact on vine health and productivity.

Despite their impact, Wine Australia said that many aspects of their biology, ecology and movement across vineyards remain poorly understood.

“Knowing exactly which pests are present will help improve understanding of how they behave in vineyards and support the development of more precise and effective biological control strategies,” explained Dr Paul Smith, general manager research and innovation at Wine Australia.

Bai said that she is extremely grateful to Wine Australia and honoured to be receiving the award alongside her PhD top up scholarship.

“With the guidance of my collaborators, I hope to provide the Australian wine industry with science-based strategies to manage these challenging pests more effectively,” she said.

Seven candidates, including Bai, have also received Wine Australia top-up scholarships in 2026.

An additional project-based PhD scholarship has also been awarded to Jiaoying Qiao (Ying) for his research into the use of artificial intelligence in advisory services, which is being carried out in collaboration with the One Basin Cooperative Research Centre.

“Maintaining strong research capability is essential to the long-term success of the Australian wine sector.

“This round of scholarships supports emerging researchers whose work will address real world challenges for grapegrowers and winemakers, while strengthening collaboration between the sector and our leading research institutions.”

Are you a Daily Wine News subscriber? If not, click here to join our mailing list. It’s free!

The post ANU PhD student awarded for vineyard pest research appeared first on Winetitles.

title: 200 Years of Shaping Australian Wine: Sydney Royal Wine Show Marks a Major Milestone
summary:
In 2026, the Sydney Royal Wine Show marks a 200-year milestone since wine was first entered as a class in the Agricultural Society of NSW Show. What began as a competition dominated by fortified wines has moved with the industry at every step, through Hocks, Clarets and Burgundy classes, through the table wine revolution of […]

The post 200 Years of Shaping Australian Wine: Sydney Royal Wine Show Marks a Major Milestone appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
In 2026, the Sydney Royal Wine Show marks a 200-year milestone since wine was first entered as a class in the Agricultural Society of NSW Show.

What began as a competition dominated by fortified wines has moved with the industry at every step, through Hocks, Clarets and Burgundy classes, through the table wine revolution of the 1960s and 70s, to moments where Australian wines firmly found their place on the international stage, recognised for our exceptional varietal wines. The evolution continues today, keeping up with stylistic changes in how consumers are drinking.

As the Sydney Royal calls for entries for this historic 2026 Show, it’s worth reflecting on how much the show has contributed to the advancement of Australian wine, with some notable characters at the helm, firmly committed to agricultural excellence.

 

2025 Wines being judged

 

Humble beginnings, starting in 1826

In 1826, during the fourth annual agricultural show, the Agricultural Society of NSW offered a small gold medal for the best sample of Colonial wine, not less than 10 gallons, certified on oath to be from the vintage of that year. By 1828, there were only four classes for wine, despite the colony only having a handful of serious growers. By 1902, a purpose-built exhibition space called ‘the wine kiosk’ had opened at the Agricultural Society of NSW’s home at Moore Park, fittingly between Burgundy Street and Claret Street, giving exhibitors the chance to showcase their wines. For a wine industry still establishing itself, having a dedicated permanent building at Australia’s most significant agricultural gathering was no small thing.

A full timeline of the Show’s history is available on the RAS website.

There is a myriad of fascinating stories woven throughout the rich tapestry of the show, often tracking the changes that were happening as the Australian wine industry found its own rhythm. From names such as Lindemans and Penfolds, who dominated the early years, to quiet achievers like Eliza Cox, the first recorded female exhibitor in the wine section in 1870. Cox entered three classes under the Winbourne name from her Mulgoa property: her White Frontignac took first prize in Class 241, her White Burgundy earned second prize in Class 235, and her Verdelho received an Honourable Mention in Class 234.

In 1891, the Society received the Royal prefix, becoming the Royal Agricultural Society of NSW. Its wine competition had by then been a fixture of the Royal Easter Show for decades, exhibited in the annexe to the Main Pavilion before eventually outgrowing it entirely.

The mission of the Royal Agricultural Society of improving agricultural excellence has always been at the heart of the show, and the society played a vital role in spreading viticultural knowledge. The Royal Agricultural Society’s annual publications served a serious practical purpose: advancing the knowledge and skill of Australia’s grape growers. The 1925 RAS Annual offers a vivid illustration of this mission in action, recording a lecture delivered during the Show by Mr H.L. Manuel, NSW Government Viticulturist, on the diseases of the vine.

In 1924, the committee noted the difficulty of securing judges during the February harvest period. By 1939, the show had shifted to October. Eventually, it found its permanent home in July, a time when the previous vintage is in the bottle and there is still time to act on what the judging reveals.

 

Continued evolution to keep pace with stylistic changes

1985-1995 Wine judging

The introduction of the medal system in 1961 was the most significant structural change in the show’s history, but refinement continued. The Wine Committee and the people who have chaired it have kept the show honest, evolving with stylistic changes in wine and shifting drinking trends.

The show has also shaped how Australian wine is assessed more broadly. The move to a 100-point judging system, digital tablet-based scoring for greater consistency, the establishment of the Capital City Wine Shows Committee, and regular meetings with NSW regional shows have all strengthened the national network. Each change has been made with the same purpose: to make the assessment more rigorous, the feedback more useful, and the results more meaningful for the producers who enter.

 

The passionate individuals driving the show forward

1977 Royal Easter Show – George Fairbrother and Len Evans

Few industries have produced figures who shaped it as decisively as those who led the Sydney Royal. Len Evans AO, James Halliday AM, Brian Croser AO and Iain Riggs AM all made their mark on wine assessment and quality during their tenure as Chair of Judges. Len was appointed in 1978 and held the role for more than two decades. He brought more than expertise to the judging panel: deep knowledge, rigorous standards and an infectious passion for the craft that continues to shape the show today.

2017 – Chair of Judges Sam Connew with future chair of judges Sarah Crowe, Mike De Iullis

There have been moments of evolution, such as in 1985 when a young Pam Dunsford started as an Associate Judge alongside Miss Jane MacQuitty, Wine Correspondent for The Times of London, the first woman from overseas ever nominated to the RAS judging panel. That two women of such distinction appeared on the same RAS judging panel in 1985 marked a quiet but significant turning point in the show’s history. International judges have played a role in getting our Australian wine stories out to the world since the early days. In 2016, the ladies proudly took charge: Samantha Connew became the first female Chair of Judges, Ms Michelle Bouffard was the International Judge, Lyndey Milan served as Chair of the Wine Committee, and Sally Evans was Chief Steward.

Many of today’s most respected wine judges began their judging careers as young Associate Judges, progressing over years to Panel Chairs and, in the case of a distinguished group of alumni, to chairing the show itself. The discipline of assessment shapes careers, and what judges take back to their own companies and vineyards ripples far beyond judging week. The Alumni of Judges over the last 25 years tells its own story about the leading wine professionals and the breadth of experience involved in assessing a wine as part of the Sydney Royal.

2025 Chair of Judges Mike De Iuliis discussing with panel

Current Chair of Judges, Mike De Iuliis, notes: “200 years, 31 judges from around Australia and all parts of the industry. The way that agricultural shows have helped Australian wine evolve has significantly benefited many, improving products by comparing them against the best in the business. There is a sense of pride and prestige associated with Sydney Royal. The panels of judges truly are the best in the business and the integrity throughout the judging process is of paramount importance. I’ve seen the benefits first-hand, introducing my wines to a much broader audience, having won accolades at this prestigious show.”

But it’s not all about the medals, with the show system generously giving back to the industry. Since 2013, over $275,000 has been invested to support 63 scholars, advancing the next generation’s understanding of viticulture, winemaking and wine assessment. Many of these scholars are now judges, alongside winemakers, journalists, sommeliers and retailers who gather to assess wines blindly, providing every entrant with critical, objective insights from accomplished palates they would not otherwise encounter. For smaller wineries without access to formal technical consultation, this rigorous assessment can be genuinely transformative.

 

Getting Medal-Winning Wines in Front of Consumers

2025 Chair of Wine Committee Angus Barnes at judging station

Medal-winning wines from the Sydney Royal are showcased at the Grape, Grain and Graze Festival each September, where over 1,000 producers, industry professionals and consumers gather at Sydney Showground to sample award-winning wines. Trophy winners are further celebrated at the Wine Communicators of Australia Trophy Luncheon, attended by over 300 industry professionals each year, and medal-winning wines are invited to participate in the medal showcase at the Royal Easter Show, the largest ticketed event in the Southern Hemisphere.

 

Enter the 2026 Sydney Royal Wine Show

2025 Hugh Spinaze – Associate Judge and Scholarship Recipient

A Sydney Royal medal is one of the most recognised and trusted marks in Australian wine: on shelf, on a wine list, and in the hands of a consumer making a decision. In a milestone year like this, that recognition carries more weight than ever.

The Royal Agricultural Society of NSW invites Australian wine producers to be part of history. For two centuries, the Sydney Royal Wine Show has continued to elevate the quality of Australian wine, evolving with the times, shining a light on established and emerging producers alike, and championing excellence across every vintage.

Entries open 22 April and close 20 May 2026. Enter at https://www.rasnsw.com.au/competitions/Food-Beverage-and-Produce/wine

 

The post 200 Years of Shaping Australian Wine: Sydney Royal Wine Show Marks a Major Milestone appeared first on Winetitles.

title: Orora working to maintain supply after UAE plant pause
summary:
Bottle manufacturer Orora has reassured beverage industry customers that its Saverglass business is working to minimise disruptions and maintain international supply following the decision to pause glass production at its UAE facility two weeks ago. As reported by The Australian Financial Review, the Ras al Khaimah (RAK) plant production was redirected to other sites after the conflict in the Middle East “closed shipping routes and made overland routes inaccessible”. Speaking to Daily Wine News, a spokesperson for Orora said that the RAK plant furnace has been transitioned to a hot closed-loop production, meaning that the furnace will remain warm with no bottle production taking place.

The post Orora working to maintain supply after UAE plant pause appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The facility mostly supplied glass for the North American premium and ultra-premium market. Image supplied

By Kyra Trussell

Bottle manufacturer Orora has reassured beverage industry customers that its Saverglass business is working to minimise disruptions and maintain international supply following the decision to pause glass production at its UAE facility two weeks ago.

As reported by The Australian Financial Review, the Ras al Khaimah (RAK) plant production was redirected to other sites after the conflict in the Middle East “closed shipping routes and made overland routes inaccessible”.

Speaking to Daily Wine News, a spokesperson for Orora said that the RAK plant furnace has been transitioned to a hot closed-loop production, meaning that the furnace will remain warm with no bottle production taking place.

“The RAK facility represents approximately 15% of Saverglass production capacity, with most of the recent production geared toward global premium and ultra-premium wines for the North America market. Our team are working to ensure continuity of supply to our customers and to minimise disruption.

“Like many businesses, we are experiencing the flow-on impacts from rising supply chain costs resulting from the Middle East conflict, including energy, freight and fuel costs – this is a dynamic and evolving situation, we are in direct communication where needed with customers on these impacts.

“We are working closely with our logistics partners, and where possible, we are accessing our global production network to help mitigate any impacts to supply,” they said.

The spokesperson also noted that the company is working with its logistics partners to “help mitigate any impacts from lost shipments”.

Orora’s South Australian glass facility in Gawler, which produces bottles for the Australian wine industry, is still operating as per usual.

title: Tamar Ridge to remain open amidst cellar door renovation
summary:
Tamar Ridge in Tasmania has commenced a renovation of its cellar door, a move that the winery said marks the next chapter in its ongoing commitment to “crafting exceptional cool-climate wines and visitor experiences in the Tamar Valley”. Tamar Ridge will remain open throughout the renovation period and will continue to welcome guests in a “curated, seasonal format”. Tastings and wine sales will be hosted on the outdoor terrace, however, in the event of inclement weather, guests will be welcomed indoors at the neighbouring Turner Stillhouse. The temporary format introduces a new collaboration between Tamar Ridge and Turner Stillhouse, with the two coming together to offer a joint tasting experience of Tamar Ridge wines with Turner Stillhouse spirits, paired with locally inspired cheese platters.

The post Tamar Ridge to remain open amidst cellar door renovation appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
A render of what the cellar door is intended to look like following renovations. Image supplied

Tamar Ridge in Tasmania has commenced a renovation of its cellar door, a move that the winery said marks the next chapter in its ongoing commitment to “crafting exceptional cool-climate wines and visitor experiences in the Tamar Valley”.

Tamar Ridge will remain open throughout the renovation period and will continue to welcome guests in a “curated, seasonal format”. Tastings and wine sales will be hosted on the outdoor terrace, however, in the event of inclement weather, guests will be welcomed indoors at the neighbouring Turner Stillhouse.

The temporary format introduces a new collaboration between Tamar Ridge and Turner Stillhouse, with the two coming together to offer a joint tasting experience of Tamar Ridge wines with Turner Stillhouse spirits, paired with locally inspired cheese platters.

Tamar Ridge venue manager Charlie Rayner said the renovation marks an exciting evolution for the winery while maintaining the warm, welcoming experience guests have come to expect.

“This is a really exciting moment for Tamar Ridge. While there will be a short period of change, we’re still very much open and looking forward to welcoming guests in a slightly different way over the coming months.

“The terrace tastings are a beautiful way to experience the Tamar Valley; relaxed, seasonal and close to the landscape that shapes our wines. We’re also thrilled to be collaborating even more closely with our neighbours Turner Stillhouse during this time,” he said.

The upgrade will see the space “thoughtfully reimagined to elevate the tasting experience, while remaining grounded in the winery’s Tasmanian provenance and understated, modern aesthetic”.

The cellar door will reopen in June, with Rayner expressing that the winery is looking forward to sharing a space “that feels true to Tamar Valley”.

Timed with the reopening of the cellar door are various winter experiences such as the return of the NOIR NOIR – the annual event in collaboration with Turner Stillhouse that brings together Pinot Noir, whisky and stargazing for a one-night only celebration.

The winery said that further details on the upgraded cellar door, upcoming events and experiences will be announced in the coming months.

title: New Zealand Winegrowers welcomes support for the India Free Trade Agreement
summary:
New Zealand Winegrowers has welcomed the announcement of Labour’s support for progressing a Free Trade Agreement between New Zealand and India, “reinforcing trade as a shared priority”. Philip Gregan, CEO of New Zealand Winegrowers, said maintenance of bipartisan alignment on trade is very positive as it gives exporters certainty and reinforces New Zealand’s reputation as a long-term trading partner. “India represents a significant long-term opportunity for New Zealand wine, and sustained cross-party commitment will be key to delivering a high-quality agreement with real commercial outcomes,” he said.

The post New Zealand Winegrowers welcomes support for the India Free Trade Agreement appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
New Zealand Winegrowers has shown support for progressing a Free Trade Agreement with India. Image courtesy New Zealand Winegrowers

New Zealand Winegrowers has welcomed the announcement of Labour’s support for progressing a Free Trade Agreement between New Zealand and India, “reinforcing trade as a shared priority”.

Philip Gregan, CEO of New Zealand Winegrowers, said maintenance of bipartisan alignment on trade is very positive as it gives exporters certainty and reinforces New Zealand’s reputation as a long-term trading partner.

“India represents a significant long-term opportunity for New Zealand wine, and sustained cross-party commitment will be key to delivering a high-quality agreement with real commercial outcomes,” he said.

New Zealand Winegrowers views the FTA as positive news for the industry as wineries will be able to plan with confidence around a clear, decade-long tariff reduction pathway.

While it noted that current exports to India are very modest, sitting at just under NZ $300,000 (FOB) for 2025 due to the very high tariffs, “improved market access under the FTA is expected to make India increasingly attractive to New Zealand wine exporters over the coming years”.

“We value the constructive approach taken across Parliament and the shared recognition of trade as a driver of growth, jobs, and regional prosperity,” Gregan said.

title: Riverland Wine appoints new executive officer
summary:
Riverland Wine has appointed Andrew Weeks as its new executive officer, effective Monday 27 April 2026. Riverland Wine said that Weeks brings a “deep understanding of the winegrape sector shaped by a career spanning viticulture, business management, national industry advocacy, and with a personal connection to the Riverland that goes back to his childhood in Renmark”. “The Riverland has always been central to Australia’s wine industry, and I’m proud to be returning to the region in this role. Growers and winemakers here are doing it tough, and they deserve strong, practical representation. I’m looking forward to working alongside the board, members and industry partners to push for the settings that will keep this region viable and thriving for the long term,” Weeks said.

The post Riverland Wine appoints new executive officer appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Andrew Weeks will assume the position from next Monday. Image supplied

Riverland Wine has appointed Andrew Weeks as its new executive officer, effective Monday 27 April 2026.

Riverland Wine said that Weeks brings a “deep understanding of the winegrape sector shaped by a career spanning viticulture, business management, national industry advocacy, and with a personal connection to the Riverland that goes back to his childhood in Renmark”.

“The Riverland has always been central to Australia’s wine industry, and I’m proud to be returning to the region in this role. Growers and winemakers here are doing it tough, and they deserve strong, practical representation.

“I’m looking forward to working alongside the board, members and industry partners to push for the settings that will keep this region viable and thriving for the long term,” Weeks said.

As CEO of Wine Grape Growers Australia, the national advocacy body, Week’s led the organisation’s transition to become Australian Vignerons which bestowed him with experience in policy, biosecurity, and research and extension at a national level.

As senior viticulture officer at CCW Co-operative, he provided technical advice to a group that accounted for around 10% of the nations annual grape crush. He also holds a Bachelor of Agricultural Science from the University of Adelaide and has lectured in wine business at Flinders University.

Brigid Nolan, chair of Riverland Wine, said that Andrew is exactly the right person for the role right now.

“His grounding in viticulture, his experience in national advocacy, and his genuine connection to the Riverland mean he understands both the technical and commercial realities our growers and winemakers face every day.

“We look forward to having him lead our advocacy with the energy and focus this region deserves,” she said.

Riverland Wine said that Week’s will work directly with government, regulators and industry partners to ensure the Riverland’s challenges, and its essential contribution to the national wine sector are properly understood and addressed.

His appointment follows the departure of outgoing executive officer Alex Cannon-Leyson, who finished her tenure on 20 March 2026.

Riverland Wine extended thanks to Cannon-Leyson for her dedication and significant contribution to the organisation during a period of extraordinary challenge and change.

title: WISA farewells committee members and confirms 2026 leadership
summary:
The Wine Industry Suppliers Association (WISA) has acknowledged the contribution of its departing committee members while confirming its leadership team. “We formally acknowledge the individuals contributing to our management committee and the critical role volunteer leadership plays in representing and advancing the wine value chain,” WISA said. The organisation also confirmed that Amy Bishop (DW Fox Tucker) and Anna Lindsay (Rural Freight Connections) are stepping down from the committee, with Steven Scott (Vinarchy) having resigned at the end of 2025.

The post WISA farewells committee members and confirms 2026 leadership appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The 2026 WISA committee. Image supplied

The Wine Industry Suppliers Association (WISA) has acknowledged the contribution of its departing committee members while confirming its leadership team.

“We formally acknowledge the individuals contributing to our management committee and the critical role volunteer leadership plays in representing and advancing the wine value chain,” WISA said.

The organisation also confirmed that Amy Bishop (DW Fox Tucker) and Anna Lindsay (Rural Freight Connections) are stepping down from the committee, with Steven Scott (Vinarchy) having resigned at the end of 2025.

Bishop served for over six years, including as secretary and public officer, while Scott was with WISA for four years and Lindsay for one.

“We thank all for their contribution to WISA and look forward to staying connected following the strong relationships built during their time with the association. All remain WISA members and continue to support the value chain through their respective businesses,” it clarified.

WISA said it is proud to welcome Athena Stamoulos, associate from Finlaysons, who joined the committee in April following Bishop’s departure.

“Stamoulos adds critical legal and commercial capability to WISA’s committee, supporting strong governance and future-focused decision-making.”

Executive chair Liz Schoen acknowledged that the contribution of volunteer committee members remains fundamental to WISA’s ability to deliver value to members and the broader industry.

“WISA exists to champion the wine value chain, and that only happens because of the individuals who step forward to contribute their time and expertise.

“Our committee members are not removed from the challenges of the industry, they are living them in their own businesses. That makes their contribution both practical and highly relevant.

“Following the transition of WineTech and the evolution of WISA’s operating model, the role of the committee has become even more important in shaping a sustainable and commercially relevant future for supplier businesses,” she said.

WISA affirmed that it continues to focus on strengthening its role as the voice of suppliers, with initiatives spanning advocacy, professional development, industry events and collaboration across the value chain.

“We remain committed to building stronger connections between suppliers and producers, while ensuring supplier businesses maintain influence, visibility and commercial relevance within the Australian and New Zealand wine sector,” WISA explained.

The 2026 committee is as follows:

  • Liz Schoen (WISA executive chair & Liz Schoen Consulting)
  • Eric Wilkes (Australian Wine Research Institute) – deputy chair
  • Ron van Buuren (Hydra Consulting) – treasurer
  • Romina Meloni (Lallemand)
  • Tim Stead (Amorim)
  • Melissa Raymond (Garden & Field)
  • Aaron Haw (Cork Supply)
  • Athena Stamoulos (Finlaysons)
  • Adrian Fiocchini (Seguin Moreau)

title: GIN Virus detected in Australia for the first time
summary:
Vinehealth Australia has today issued a biosecurity alert to advise the grapegrowing industry that Grapevine Berry Inner Necrosis Virus (GIN Virus/GINV/ Trichovirus necroacini) has been detected in vineyards across Victoria, Queensland and South Australia. These detections are the first time GIN Virus has been recorded and reported in Australia, however, Vinehealth said that the virus is likely to have been present in Australia for some years. It is likely that the virus propagated and distributed in plant material across eastern Australia, therefore, Vinehealth concluded that eradication is not possible. According to Vinehealth, during late 2025 and early 2026 the virus was detected in table grapevines and rootstocks that were diseased yet asymptomatic in grapegrowing regions within Victoria, Queensland and South Australia.

The post GIN Virus detected in Australia for the first time appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Vinehealth Australia has today issued a biosecurity alert to advise the grapegrowing industry that Grapevine Berry Inner Necrosis Virus (GIN Virus/GINV/ Trichovirus necroacini) has been detected in vineyards across Victoria, Queensland and South Australia.

These detections are the first time GIN Virus has been recorded and reported in Australia, however, Vinehealth said that the virus is likely to have been present in Australia for some years. It is likely that the virus propagated and distributed in plant material across eastern Australia, therefore, Vinehealth concluded that eradication is not possible.

According to Vinehealth, during late 2025 and early 2026 the virus was detected in table grapevines and rootstocks that were diseased yet asymptomatic in grapegrowing regions within Victoria, Queensland and South Australia.

Asymptomatic mature grapevines samples from South Australian table grapes returned positive results to GIN Virus in 2026. The extent of the virus spread is currently unknown.

Vinehealth Australia explained that some infected vines in Australia displayed restricted growth in Spring, however, it is not possible to confirm if this was due to the virus or other contributing factors causing the reduced growth. As the season progresses, vines may grow out and appear normal. Asymptomatic infections also occur, meaning an infected vine may not show any visual symptoms of the virus.

“The disease is established in China and Japan where it is known to impact the health and productivity of sensitive varieties. Overseas experience has shown that symptoms vary across different varieties.

“Leaf symptoms have been recorded in some GIN Virus infected wine and table grapes and rootstocks, but asymptomatic infections also occur. Impacts on fruit production are only recorded in table grapes. Although some winegrape varieties may show leaf symptoms, it is unknown if GIN Virus will significantly impact fruit quality and yield or sensory differences on resulting wines.

“Visual symptoms can include delayed budburst, stunted growth, zig zag shoots and affected shoots may have internal necrosis. Leaves may be smaller and can show yellow blotches, mosaic-like patterns or small rings. Bunches may be more compact and unusually tight, berries may be smaller, ripen slowly, and have reduced grape quality including reduced sugar and acid balance. Berries can have inner necrosis, which gives the virus its name, but can also exhibit necrosis on the outside,” Vinehealth Australia detailed in the alert.

Currently, laboratory analysis is the only method known to identify GIN Virus.

“The virus can also be transmitted via grafting of infected planting material. The grape erineum mite (blister and bud mite) Colomerus vitis transmits the GIN Virus between grapevines.”

There is no known cure for infected vines and no published information on the economic impact of GIN Virus at this stage. The speed and risk of spread is also currently unknown.

“Site quarantine action plans for the infected properties in Australia are being refined. It is expected the infected properties will be required to undertake strict farmgate hygiene to prevent the spread to other adjoining and contact vineyards.

“Industry is conducting further investigations and surveillance to better understand this virus and how it can be managed to minimise the impact,” Vinehealth Australia explained.

To prevent GIN Virus entering the vineyard, Vinehealth encourages the prioritisation of farmgate hygiene by practicing ‘clean in and clean out’. Controlling vectors/mites can help slow the spread to adjoining vines.

Grapegrowers should purchase known GIN Virus-free planting material and ask for evidence of testing.

“Various chemical, biological and cultural control methods in line with integrated pest management can be adopted to encourage predatory insects into your vineyard. Check with the AWRI ‘Dog Book’ Agrochemicals Booklet for current chemical strategies for mite control. Some fungicides will reduce predator populations and allow mites to flourish, increasing the risk of GIN Virus spread.  Use insecticides in line with good Integrated Pest Management practices (IPM).

“It is unclear if other insect vectors, such as other mites, scale and mealybugs, may spread the virus. Additional vectors may include people, plant, soil, machinery and equipment where insect vectors may hitchhike on these items.”

If a vineyard has GIN Virus, the workers should prioritise farmgate hygiene, use clean grapevine material, work in uninfected vineyards before moving to infected vineyards, and clean machinery between vineyards to prevent the spread.

Overseas, GIN Virus has been known to infect winegrape varieties, although there is no evidence in Australia that winegrapes have been infected thus far.

Virus testing can be undertaken at:

Agriculture Victoria: (03) 9032 7515 or chs.reception@agriculture.vic.gov.au

New South Wales Department of Primary Industries and Regional Development: 1800 675 623 or laboratory.services@dpird.nsw.gov.au

Vinehealth Australia encourages anyone with questions to contact them on (08) 8273 0550 or email admin@vinehealth.com.au, or call the Vinehealth Australia CEO, Dr Samantha Scarratt, on 0436 415 611.

Vinehealth Australia said it will update South Australian vineyard owners once further information is known.

Are you a Daily Wine News subscriber? If not, click here to join our mailing list. It’s free!

The post GIN Virus detected in Australia for the first time appeared first on Winetitles.

title: Calabria unifies domestic distribution under House of Fine Wine
summary:
Calabria Family Wine Group has confirmed House of Fine Wine as its unified domestic trading and distribution platform. The group said that this creates a simpler, more streamlined way for the market to engage with the group’s portfolio. The move follows the group’s acquisition of House of Fine Wine and The Wine Company back in February, “marking the next phase of integration, bringing its domestic distribution businesses together under a single, premium, market-facing identity”.

The post Calabria unifies domestic distribution under House of Fine Wine appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
House of Fine Wine will be Calabria’s unified domestic trading and distribution platform. Image supplied

Calabria Family Wine Group has confirmed House of Fine Wine as its unified domestic trading and distribution platform.

The group said that this creates a simpler, more streamlined way for the market to engage with the group’s portfolio.

The move follows the group’s acquisition of House of Fine Wine and The Wine Company back in February, “marking the next phase of integration, bringing its domestic distribution businesses together under a single, premium, market-facing identity”.

The winery said that by operating under one banner, it will strengthen its national reach and capability across on-premise and independent retail channels.

Third-generation family member Andrew Calabria, sales and marketing director, said the move reflects the group’s long-term vision for a more cohesive and scalable distribution model.

“This is a natural and exciting next step following our recent acquisitions. By bringing our distribution businesses together under the House of Fine Wine name, we’re creating greater clarity for the market while building a platform that supports growth, investment and long-term partnerships, all while remaining proudly family owned,” he said.

Simon Limmer, CEO of Indevin Group, owner of the Villa Maria brand, said the unified platform will support the brands continued growth in Australia.

“Calabria Family Wine Group brings deep market expertise, with a strong distribution platform that ensures greater consistency in how brands are represented.

“We’re confident this next phase will strengthen execution for Villa Maria in Australia and support continued investment in long-term brand building with the trade,” he said.

title: TWE flags operational changes amidst company restructure
summary:
In an ASX announcement, Treasury Wine Estates (TWE) has today announced the transition to a new “regional operating model” amidst the progression of its global transformation program, TWE Ascent, aiming to “position the business for long-term sustainable growth”. The regional operating model will be structured across the Americas, Australia and New Zealand (ANZ), Europe, Greater China, and emerging markets such as the rest of Asia, Middle East and Africa. It will come into effect 1 October 2026. TWE said it is focusing on creating a unified, simplified portfolio for “increased focus and execution at a market level”. Further details will be provided at TWE’s Investors Day on 4 June 2026.

The post TWE flags operational changes amidst company restructure appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The winery has announced its new regional operating model. Image courtesy Penfolds

In an ASX announcement, Treasury Wine Estates (TWE) has today announced the transition to a new “regional operating model” amidst the progression of its global transformation program, TWE Ascent, aiming to “position the business for long-term sustainable growth”.

The regional operating model will be structured across the Americas, Australia and New Zealand (ANZ), Europe, Greater China, and emerging markets such as the rest of Asia, Middle East and Africa. It will come into effect 1 October 2026.

TWE said it is focusing on creating a unified, simplified portfolio for “increased focus and execution at a market level”. Further details will be provided at TWE’s Investors Day on 4 June 2026.

 

Market depletions

The company has seen “improved depletions performance in 3Q26”, noting “strong depletions growth” in China “driven by the ongoing demand for Bin 389 and Bin 407”. TWE said that performance through the Chinese New Year period was a highlight, with “depletions up 40% in China on a seasonally adjusted basis and momentum continuing to the end of 3Q26”.

In ANZ, 3Q26 depletions grew 11% and in Asia (excluding China), depletions grew 14% on a seasonally adjusted basis according to TWE.

Treasury Americas overall US depletions grew 9.1% in 3Q26, “reflecting improved momentum”.

The winery said that the positive depletions momentum is expected to support its focus on reducing customer inventory levels in China and the US.

 

Funding update

TWE said that as a part of its ongoing funding program, it has “established new debt commitments” totalling $300 million “from a number of lenders within its global banking group”. The company said these commitments will be used to refinance F27 debt maturities and further increase TWE’s liquidity position, which it claims is expected to exceed $1 billion by the end of F26. The winery said it retains “significant headroom to the financial covenants under its borrowing arrangements and remains confident in its ability to return leverage to the target 1.5-2.0x range”.

TWE CEO Sam Fischer said that the company is reshaping to drive clearer accountability for performance and to enable faster, more market-connected decision-making.

“Combining the deep local insight of our in-market teams with the scale and expertise of our global functions will step change in-market execution, whilst retaining our enhanced focus on Penfolds and other priority luxury brands.

“I am pleased with the progress we are making on elevating our focus on depletions performance across our key markets, and we remain focused on continuing the improved momentum,” he said.

 

‘Executive Leadership Team’ changes

TWE will realign its ‘Executive Leadership Team’ (ELT), driving the organisation transition to the new operating model, effective 1 October 2026.

Tom King, who is currently managing director for Penfolds, will assume a new position as chief commercial officer. He will report to CEO Sam Fischer and lead the regional sales, marketing, direct to consumer and commercial strategy activities for all regions except the Americas. He will be based in Melbourne.

Reporting to Tom King, Angus Lilley, who is currently managing director for Treasury Collective, will lead the ANZ and Europe regions “with accountability for regional sales and marketing activities” for TWE’s focused brand portfolio from Melbourne.

Jack Wu, currently general manager Mainland China for Penfolds, will lead the Greater China region, based in Shanghai.

Kirsty Keyte, current chief marketing officer for Penfolds, will become the new position of chief marketing and innovation officer. She will be based in Melbourne and report to Tom King, being responsible for global marketing and the delivery of global brand strategy.

Also reporting to Fischer will be Ben Dollard, current president of Treasury Americas. He will lead TWE’s Americas region with responsibility for sales, marketing, direct to consumer and commercial strategy activities.

“The direct CEO reporting line reflects heightened focus on TWE’s Americas business, providing direct line of sight into in-market execution in the Americas. A dotted reporting line into the CCO ensures a consistent and disciplined execution framework across all regions including the Americas,” said TWE.

Kerrin Petty will remain chief supply and sustainability officer, with an expanded global supply remit, including Americas supply. Petty will report to Fischer and be based in Adelaide.

Group functions reporting to CEO Fischer include the chief financial and strategy officer, chief legal and corporate relations officer (incorporating the legal, corporate affairs and company secretariat functions), chief people officer and chief digital and technology officer.

Are you a Daily Wine News subscriber? If not, click here to join our mailing list. It’s free!

 

The post TWE flags operational changes amidst company restructure appeared first on Winetitles.

title: ANU PhD student awarded for vineyard pest research
summary:
A new project led by Australian National University (ANU) PhD candidate Yilin Bai is targeting two significant pests to Australian vineyards. Bai has subsequently been awarded Wine Australia’s Dr Tony Jordan OAM Award for 2026. Named in honour of the late Dr Tony Jordan OAM, the award recognises “the most outstanding applicant of Wine Australia’s annual PhD and Master by Research top-up scholarship program”. A key part of Bai’s research is developing an easy and reliable way to accurately identify scale insect and mealybug species using machine learning and genetic tools. The project will also use genomic methods to track how the pests spread across vineyards.

The post ANU PhD student awarded for vineyard pest research appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Yinlin Bai, the PhD candidate leading the pest research. Image supplied

A new project led by Australian National University (ANU) PhD candidate Yilin Bai is targeting two significant pests to Australian vineyards.

Bai has subsequently been awarded Wine Australia’s Dr Tony Jordan OAM Award for 2026. Named in honour of the late Dr Tony Jordan OAM, the award recognises “the most outstanding applicant of Wine Australia’s annual PhD and Master by Research top-up scholarship program”.

A key part of Bai’s research is developing an easy and reliable way to accurately identify scale insect and mealybug species using machine learning and genetic tools. The project will also use genomic methods to track how the pests spread across vineyards.

Wine Australia described how scale insects and mealybugs can damage grapevines by feeding on plant sap, which can reduce vine vigour and slow growth. As the pests feed, they excrete honeydew that coats leaves and grape bunches, encouraging sooty mould which reduces photosynthesis and contaminates fruit.

This contamination can be devastating, leading to downgraded or rejected grapes at harvest. Scale insects and mealybugs are also known vectors of grapevine viruses, further compounding their potential impact on vine health and productivity.

Despite their impact, Wine Australia said that many aspects of their biology, ecology and movement across vineyards remain poorly understood.

“Knowing exactly which pests are present will help improve understanding of how they behave in vineyards and support the development of more precise and effective biological control strategies,” explained Dr Paul Smith, general manager research and innovation at Wine Australia.

Bai said that she is extremely grateful to Wine Australia and honoured to be receiving the award alongside her PhD top up scholarship.

“With the guidance of my collaborators, I hope to provide the Australian wine industry with science-based strategies to manage these challenging pests more effectively,” she said.

Seven candidates, including Bai, have also received Wine Australia top-up scholarships in 2026.

An additional project-based PhD scholarship has also been awarded to Jiaoying Qiao (Ying) for his research into the use of artificial intelligence in advisory services, which is being carried out in collaboration with the One Basin Cooperative Research Centre.

“Maintaining strong research capability is essential to the long-term success of the Australian wine sector.

“This round of scholarships supports emerging researchers whose work will address real world challenges for grapegrowers and winemakers, while strengthening collaboration between the sector and our leading research institutions.”

Are you a Daily Wine News subscriber? If not, click here to join our mailing list. It’s free!
